body:
I think it's a good idea to align the implems for 2 reasons: 

1. Complexity
2. Generally, perf is worse when collaborating. And it's much more likely we'll notice perf issues locally, thus more likely we will do something about it, rather than if it's hidden under collab.

(2) actually relates to everything, like noticing there are bugs in the collab implem only.

let's distinguish between an `update` and `edit`. In here we're tracking when a user clicks on the edit button, but doesn't necessarily update. 

I didn't add a separate `update` event as I don't think it's useful knowledge. Technically even the edit isn't, if we don't distinguish how the user edits (i.e. which button they used), so we wouldn't be able to figure out which UI is being used more often.

We should prob remove this altogether or track the actual UI button.

Now it works... but I simply removed check for isMobile || isIPad.
It turns out `export const isIPad = /iPad/.test(window.navigator.platform);` resolves to false on iOS 15.1 (in my case).
I see no negative effect of removing the check for mobile and iPad and allowing link click on desktop as well. Why was it limited to isMobile?
author: zsviczian

body: @dwelle, btw, my finding above also suggests that probably isDarwin resolves to false on iOS15.1 as well. Back to the conversation from yesterday, these device checks are flimsy. Maybe would be better to use a package like [platform.js](https://www.npmjs.com/package/platform) - thought this one seems to be dead, it was last updated two years ago.
author: zsviczian

body:
> I see no negative effect of removing the check for mobile and iPad and allowing link click on desktop as well. Why was it limited to isMobile?

Yes, link-click can have the threshold on desktop as well, but on the link icon only (not the element body — which on Mobile is disabled, anyway)

> Maybe would be better to use a package like [platform.js](https://www.npmjs.com/package/platform) - thought this one seems to be dead, it was last updated two years ago.

I'm not sure, and as you said, it's not up-to-date. It purportedly [detects iPad as Mac](https://github.com/bestiejs/platform.js/issues/194).
author: dwelle

body:
@ad1992, why are we checking `isMobile` in `isPointHittingLinkIcon`? Is that really necessary. With the last change I practically disabled that check. I modified isIPad to `isIPad = true` and pass `this.isMobile || isPad` to isPointHittingLinkIcon in every case.... and I can't spot any negative effect on the desktop.

Thus my recommendation for this PR is to remove isMobile from `isPointHittingLinkIcon` and to remove the check for isMobile from `private handleCanvasPointerUp`.
author: zsviczian

body: The `isMobile` check in `isPointHittingLinkIcon` was added to make sure that clicking on element takes to link only in view mode in non mobile devices, this means that for `ipad` also in view mode the element will be clickable.

body: See my comment above. I set this to true because I had a hunch that it does not resolve correctly for iOS15.1 (and my hunch proved to be correct. Setting this to fixed true the links started to work on my iOS15.1 iPad as well.

body:
I don't think we should be using this API internally as it was designed for host app purposes. Right now it doesn't hurt, but long-term we might make it so that `updateScene` doesn't notify host apps etc.

We should figure out how to make it clear that these APIs are for host app consumption only.
author: dwelle

body:
let's use all elements under cursor (`getElementsAtPosition()`), not just the top-most. This will fix two major cases:

1) elements covering each other

![eraser_zindex](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5153846/157744134-6e4127ff-49f5-4e1b-bbb0-9133b2487ad7.gif)

2) elements (lines etc.) very close to each other but not covering each other, especially when moving the cursor fast so that there aren't many samples which to hitbox
author: dwelle

body: I'd not mutate the event because you never know where it'll be used after this function calls (potentially even later in this function). Let's just keep the positions as local variables
author: dwelle

body:
- you're hitbox-testing the original position twice (once in previous invocation, once at the start of the loop)
- I'd also check if the loop if the interpolation is needed at all. First, I'd go from end to start: drop the `samplingInterval` variable and just use the `distance`, starting from the end and subtracting `threshold` from it on each loop iteration, and making the loop condition to be `distance >= threshold`. This way you don't loop unnecessarily and reduce the variables.
author: dwelle

body: hitbox-testing original position twice ? I am hitbox testing the last coords here and scene pointer at the end of the loop as the condition is `sampleInterval < distance`, I can club that into the loop as well using `sampleInterval <= distance`. However as per the  `sampleInterval` if we go in reverse direction we will still need a temp variable to reduce distance by threshold since we will need to calculate the ratio of current distance to actual distance

Doing a `: File | undefined` is one of the rare cases where a type declaration is less typesafe than doing a type assertion `as File | undefined`, because TS will narrow down the assignment to `File` since it's inferred as such.

But, I've changed it to using `.item()` API instead which has the correct type we need.

body:
> Also I am not sure why we don't show the actions when elements selected and clicked on canvas coz when user is selecting elements they want to use the actions on those elements so it makes more intutive to show those actions even when clicked on canvas

That cannot be the assumption. The standard is that clicking on canvas means you want canvas actions. Some apps even auto-deselect when click on canvas, and we _may_ wanna do so later as well.

Either way, the way Excalidraw currently works is that clicking on canvas _does not_ show element actions, no matter of selection, so we shouldn't break that assumption just for this action.

How were you matching the key? If using our `KEYS.L`, then that won't work because holding the `Shift` capitalizes it (`event.key` is a printable character). So you'd have to test for capital L (or use `event.code`, but we should use `event.key` whenever possible).

> We can't have `Cmd+L` for the shortcut because that's a browser shortcut (at least in chrome) for focusing the address bar.

Btw, this event is preventable so we could override the browser behavior. Miro does that as they use `Cmd/Ctrl-L` for locking.

Figma/tldraw uses `Cmd-Shift-L`. I suppose we should use it too.
author: dwelle

- Hold Cmd - keyTest is called

body:
The `updateLibrary` is a high-level API, same as `updateScene`, which also takes `appState` (which can alter UI state) and `commitToHistory`. It's about usefulness and semantics. Since opening the library menu is currently tied to updating the library, it makes sense to expose it.

The alternative is to force host apps to do an extra:

```
excalidrawAPI.updateScene({ appState: { isLibraryOpen: true } });
```

I don't care much, but I think the argument here and in your other comments stems from wanting to make this API low-level, which we don't need to.
author: dwelle

body:
I see. This is to keep the current behavior. 

Library UI is still handled by us (and even later we'll just factor it out to a reusable component). Forcing host apps to handle this part, which is kinda integral to it, is not good (especially once we move to an on-brand confirmation dialog). Also they'd lose i18n.

We can rename to `askForConfirmation`.

body:
Because we normalise the newlines potentially from `
` -> `
` before rendering we assume that newlines are removed here. Otherwise every time we hit a `
` our character color is going to be offset by one.

I think we should change this so that `TextColorRanges` tracks the color of `` and `
`, we can then account for this in renderElement.
author: tom-sherman

body: This is also causing a bug in renderElement where the selected stroke is off by some offset depending on how many newlines you have.
author: tom-sherman

body:
We have a problem with grapheme clusters or multi-codepoint-letters or whatever you want to call them. The core issue with the current code is that  `[...'🤌🏼👌🏿']` evaluates to `[ '🤌', '🏼', '👌', '🏿' ]` 

https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9257001/167246490-05ecd607-74e9-4d6d-ab9a-741cbb0d01a6.mov
author: tom-sherman

body:
I'm assuming that the text selection API also works on graphemes ie. selecting `🤌🏼` gives you a range of length 1 not 2. If this is the case I think the only solution is to have both `textElement.colorRanges` and `getLineGroupedRanges` work off of graphemes too.

In any case somewhere is going to have to be responsible for splitting by grapheme which probably means we need to pull in https://github.com/orling/grapheme-splitter

Edit: Text selection doesn't work on graphemes. Selecting any standard emoji gives you a range of length 2.
author: tom-sherman

body:
Ah, we should use https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Intl/Segmenter

Would need to include a polyfill for firefox tho, not sure on the strategy of supporty/polyfills in this repo. Can anyone comment?
author: tom-sherman

body:
haven't reviewed yet so unclear what issues you're facing exactly, but do we even need to be aware of graphemes? Can't we just save the exact offsets of a given selection range? I assume the answer is we can't mainly due to how it would work alongside line breaks when bound text comes into play?

If not, then the key is not to use array spread `[...string]` because it splits on code points (thus is potentially multi-byte), and instead use `string.split('')` which splits on bytes — then you can just index into the string as normal.
author: dwelle

body: The problem with doing `.split('')` here is that once they're split it's not clear how to recombine the codepoints to form the original graphemes. This is also why we need to be aware of graphemes in the first place, not sure you can apply a colour range without worrying about them because a range applies to graphemes - not codepoints.
author: tom-sherman

body:
Depending on what you do afterwards, you should still be able to treat the string as a black box and it won't really matter?

For example, if you have `Peru 🇵🇪`, and the user selected the flag emoji, the range would be `[5, 9]` (the flag is 4 bytes). You split which gets you:

```
const bytes = ['P', 'e', 'r', 'u', ' ', '\uD83C', '\uDDF5', '\uD83C', '\uDDEA']
```

You extract the flag by slicing it out:

```
const selection = bytes.slice(5, 9);
```

You can then `selection.join('')` which gets you the 🇵🇪 grapheme back.
author: dwelle

body: Hmmm, for some reason I had the idea that `str.split('').join('') === str` wasn't always true.
author: tom-sherman

body: Seems to work fine in practice though and this suggestion definitely makes sense intuitively. I've implemented it in e7d5bfa37d17ff38a5a29793a7188eca153c0f90

body:
The default of `{}` is applied inside of `newTextElement`. I think `colorRanges` should be either optional _or_ required in both `newTexrElement` _and_ `createElement` for consistency, therefore I don't think it makes sense to apply the default here and we can instead pass the `undefined` value through to be handled by `newTextElement`.

Or have I misunderstood your point?
author: tom-sherman

body: I believe this `createElement` method is for `ExcalidrawElement`s in general, and `colorRanges` is for `ExcalidrawTextElement`s (and/or custom `ExcalidrawElement`s which generalize `ExcalidrawTextElement`). So, you may want to omit this line and handle `colorRanges` from the `...rest` parameters instead.

It will make users think they need to close it themselves when they don't. Adds friction. If you want to make the message closable even despite a timer, let's add an on-click handler that will close it (when `closable=true`), but let's not render an explicit button.

You can make a case for host apps (let them do whatever they want), but I think we're prematurely overcomplicating the API.

To improve it a bit, we could change it to:

tags:

body:
I'm actually not sure this is the best UX. `stopCollaboration` is the same as if you went to the collab dialog and clicked on "stop session". It removes the link from url, and reverts the whiteboard to your previous localStorage content. So the tab effectively becomes a regular, non-room tab.

And on reconnect it goes through the connect-to-room flow again (loads the link, loads the content...). In prod the reconnect itself is not good UX, because loading the data from firestore takes time.

I think we should implement a custom disconnect/reconnect logic for this use case alone.

body:
this function must be optimized as it can take 150+ ms per frame for 50-point line (common in libraries) when called in the pointermove handler to determine which (mid)point you're hovering over — which causes noticeable lag, even race conditions where points tend to stay in hover state even after you move the cursor away.

If we can't make the algo better, e.g. narrow down to the segment the cursor is in instead of computing cursor distance to every line segment center, then we'll need to precalculate all segment midpoints and cache on line editor construction (it would also need to update on point move/delete/add/etc.).

body:
I agree with both of you. We definitely need to fix this issue. But having two props for theme (`props.theme` and `props.defaultTheme`) is not great, even though `defaultTheme` does solve this problem.

What we want is the ability to have the theme controlled (`props.theme`) but also show the UI toggle, _and_ still have the DX ok-ish.

So I suggest we keep the `props.theme` behavior as is, but make the (already existing) `props.UIOptions.canvasOptions.theme` to work in this way: default to `undefined` which means we'll render the theme toggle if `props.theme` is `undefined`. If `false`, never render it. If `true`, always render it regardless of `props.theme` existence.

We'll also want to factor out the theme persistence from `appState.theme` (`STORAGE_KEYS.LOCAL_STORAGE_APP_STATE`) to `theme` (`STORAGE_KEYS.THEME`). Not strictly necessary, but we want to be able to quickly access the theme without having to import the whole state (`importFromLocalStorage()`). App theme is semantically separate from editor theme anyway.

My initial intention was to test the flip pixel by pixel of the canvas before and after. 

For now tests are only covering the correctness of the bounding box of the objects before and after. It will be cool to test that the actual flipping occurred correctly graphically, but I wasn't able to do it in jest. 
Other improvements of the tests include:
- clearing local storage before each test.
- take into account objects that have curve inside/outside min/max points bounds.
- differentiate between 2 point and multipoint linear elements.

body:
The `LinearElementEditor.pointerDownState.segmentMidpoint` should be part of global `pointerDownState`, or possibly the whole `LinearElementEditor.pointerDownState` should be. Because if we're updating `LinearElementEditor.pointerDownState` in a way that requires it to be saved back to `appState` (instead of mutating it), there's a race condition unless we `flushSync` it everywhere we update the linear editor — but even then I'm sure it'd be error prone.

The whole flow we're handling state during pointerdown/pointermove is super brittle and subject to race conditions right now. This manifests in things like:

![excal-midpoint-creation-loop](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/5153846/204149460-2bdd463c-5bf3-48e2-a68b-628427330f82.gif)

Wrapping these calls into `flushSync` seems to fix it, but there are many other places where we do not set (linear editor) state synchronously, so those places may be subject to race conditions as well.
author: dwelle

body:
Hey, can you share the exact steps to reproduce this? For me adding midpoints inside the editor works fine.

As per mutation yes we are using `flushSync` in a few places where the updated state needs to be used but though it's a hacky fix, we will need to rewrite it properly so we can take advantage of batching
author: ad1992

body:
Oh, forgot to mention, but it may not work for you even with these steps. The idea is (I think) to introduce enough lag into the pointermouse handler so that the state doesn't update before the next pointermove handler fires.

For what, all I needed to do is:
1) add a console.log after the `addMidPoint()` call in `App.tsx`
2) enter line editor
3) drag to add midpoint

You can try adding more logs if one doesn't work for you.

body:
what's the reason for filtering out MenuGroups? At best it'll slow things down, and at worst it'll prevent nested MenuGroups.

That said, right now the reason for `<MenuGroup>` compo is only so that apps can render a group of items since we filter every non-whitelisted nodes out in `getValidMenuChildren()` (that "arbitrary" gap on the group should be removed, and added in host).

I'd retain the top-level filtering (and thus `MenuGroup` compo) only if we decide to have a hybrid solution where we render default items together with custom ones (where if people wanted not to render a default item, they'd need to render it and pass `render={false}` to it) — in which case having named components will be necessary (almost). This would serve as a solution to #6023 case 1 (the problem of host apps having to default items in sync) instead of having "default blocks"). But then we'd either have to recursively gather children (going down into MenuGroups) or tell host apps that if they want to put default menu items inside MenuGroups then they also need to render them in top-level with `render={false}` prop. They'd potentially have to do it anyway in case they'll render into submenus (once we support them).

Otherwise can let hosts render whatever they want.
author: dwelle

body:
Yeah, but we can't start breaking the component contract if you're rendering something tiny different from a plain string inside the item.

We also don't want to be highlighting the item if it's not really a list item, as we're doing with the `<LanguageList/>` item.

So I'm wondering if `DropdownMenu` is the right choice here, and whether we don't want a plain `Popover`.

Right now this is an internal implementation as we don't expose the DropdownMenu to users, so we can refactor later. But we'll need to come up with a public API for the menu items so that users can either use an item that should act as a list item (what `DropdownMenuItem` does right now when you supply a plain string) and when it should.. do almost nothing at all I suppose (I'm not sure what Menu styling/behavior we want to retain on items like our LanguageList item)?

---

I think for now we'll tell users that if they want to use items other than `MainMenu.Item`, then they should use `MainMenu.Group` or whatever they want. And we'll document what classes they should use to maintain padding/gaps/etc. correctly.
author: dwelle

body:
I'm thinking it over and we really should go with the smallest API surface area we can now. Or not necessarily, but try not to be too generic because we don't really know what we're building yet. What we need to support.

So let's just expose the things we use ourselves. And if we think our use case is too specific, let's not export it.

And even then we'll end up breaking great many things anyway.
author: dwelle

body:
I don't know how to abstract this wrapper yet, so I didn't.

One idea is to split `MainMenu.Item` into `MainMenu.ListItem` (the ones with `:hover` states, `onClick` handlers etc.) and `MainMenu.GenericItem`, latter of which would use the style of the div wrapper above. But honestly, the `GenericItem` isn't really generic item, it'd be just a container with some styling.

Another idea is to have different contexts for items. The list items would be the same `MainMenu.Item` components, but wrapped into a `<MainMenu.List>` which would style them as list items. But the issue here is that the styled div above again isn't an Item really, so we couldn't reuse the Item component for it as there aren't `onClick` handlers and such.

body:
I didn't get it, what's the worry in letting users render whatever they want inside the menu item and not limit it to just a button/link in the main menu? 
I think we should give the flexibility to the host to render any valid children inside the menu items and once we start / if we start supporting more elements eg check box then users can use those but until then its ok for them to render

As per deciding whether to render a button or link, I think we should have `MenuItem.link` instead if the user wants to render a link and not decide based on `onClick` or `link` property. 
So I think it's better to export `MainMenu.Item.Link` and later we can export more as we go. 
As per the fallback, I think we should keep it so the host can still render other valid HTML elements which we don't support and in docs we are recommending using `MenuItem.Item`.
We can even default to always `button` but without `onClick` handler `button` doesn't make sense hence the fallback to just act as a wrapper for custom component.
Or to simplify more if we don't want to fallback, we can export `Menu.Item.Custom` which would add the wrapper and rest all taken care by host as now.

>  think for now we'll tell users that if they want to use items other than MainMenu.Item, then they should use MainMenu.Group or whatever they want. And we'll document what classes they should use to maintain padding/gaps/etc. correctly.

I don't think documenting classes is a good idea, those are implementation details and the host should never depend on them or need to know about them.


> I'm thinking it over and we really should go with the smallest API surface area we can now. Or not necessarily, but try not to be too generic because we don't really know what we're building yet. What we need to support.

Do you mean reintroduce filtering ?

I think we should give the flexibility to the host to render any valid children inside the menu items and once we start / if we start supporting more elements eg check box then users can use those but until then its ok for them to render

That's what I'm saying. Let users render what they want. Current implem doesn't allow that. We're rendering three different DOM types and we're ignoring the props on the fallback.

So, yes, let's split up, but a flat structure:

- `MainMenu.Item` or `MainMenu.ItemButton` or `MainMenu.Button`
- `MainMenu.ItemLink` or just `MainMenu.Link`
- `MainMenu.ItemCustom` or just `MainMenu.Custom`

body:
Thanks for decoupling #6080 from #6037, it makes it easier to reason about the implementation! 

Here is the tl;dr of all the referenced PRs since it took me a while to wrap my head around:
- #6080 - registers additional custom actions to be called from the context menu; removes existing actions by name.
- #5983 - exposes existing actions to be called externally
- #6008 - exposes custom context menu renderer function to have full control over UI and actions content/order.

In my view listed above PRs complement each other by serving different use cases and can be treated/merged separately.
author: valgussev

body: You might want to plug in `isActionEnabled` check into `_openContextMenu` function in order to make sure disabled by `registerDisableFn` call actions are not added into `ContextMenu`. Otherwise we will see those actions available in the context menu but click won't do anything.
author: valgussev

body:
this is hardcoding all custom actions to be rendered in the properties sidebar which is arbitrary. And we will be removing component rendering from actions anyway: https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/issues/6075

As such, rendering will need to be handled by host once we add support for rendering stuff to all manner of components such as the properties sidebar. https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/issues/6142
author: dwelle

body:
tying the custom contextMenu to the toolbar is also arbitrary coupling. Right now we don't even support contextMenu other than on canvas, but it's something we may consider as part of https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/issues/6139.

That said, for your specific case of switching between toolbar tool types, it should be implemented by a tool dropdown, see https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/issues/6140
author: dwelle

body: we should add shortcut labels into the Action object itself — for now. Ultimately we'll switch to shortcut parsing so we can specify both the label and the actual shortcut as a string (thus deduplicate the label).
author: dwelle

body: rendering custom contextMenu will have to be done differently https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/issues/6139
author: dwelle

body: `ActionManager.getCustomActions` filters out custom actions, including by predicates and action guards in `ActionManager.isActionEnabled`.  I suppose the caller of `getCustomActions` should have the discretion to call `isActionEnabled`.

body: I don't mean `getCustomActions` here, but the rendering into the sidebar.

body: I agree about using tool dropdowns.  My specific case does not involve switching between tool types.  Rather, I permit configuring seldom-modified properties of a single tool.  E.g. I use LaTeX on some boards and AsciiMath on other boards.  But I don't use both on the same board.

body:
> We don't but if its used in the component API then we should document unless its some hidden property which we don't recommend to use.

Nothing against that, but the `isSidebarDocked` wasn't introduced in this PR, and the new docs aren't written yet.

> Moreover why. a component shouldn't depend on Excalidraw app State ? This sort of is confusing or may be I am missing something
can we not make it controlled in that case so it's completely controlled by host via a prop /callback?
I think you have mentioned something in regards to this but I am still not clear so would be great if you could elaborate.

They can depend on Excalidraw state. This case is different that the default Sidebar is both controlled via appState and the `docked` prop. So as per the notes above, we can either make it (1) semi-controlled or (2) fully controlled.

The (1) didn't pan out because we wouldn't be able to properly update appState based on `props.docked` (and it also seems like a bad pattern).

So I went with (2), renaming the appState to make it clear it's just a user preference, and not necessarily reflect the actual state.

body:
moved to feature as filing under breaking change was a mistake. There's no breaking change with this API.

That said, we should be structuring our breaking changes differently, and if nothing else, list them first and that's the most important thing for host apps when reading a changelog.
author: dwelle

body:
> That said, we should be structuring our breaking changes differently, and if nothing else, list them first and that's the most important thing for host apps when reading a changelog.

When breaking change is as result of some feature / fix then it should be a sub heading so its clear why it was introduced and how to deal with it instead of separating features/fixes and breaking change separately. Even when its a subheading its going to catch the attention as well and also explain the context better due to main heading.

EDIT: Thinking again since we have been pushed breaking changes to make the API better in that case its not good to split it across multiple headings so I will club into one heading "Breaking Changes" instead so all breaking changes are at one place

body: It is actually used like that in some other instances too, so for consistency's sake, I've not changed this for now.
author: barnabasmolnar

body:
Sure.

Please refer to https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/pull/6483#issuecomment-1521566179 for the gist of it and some of the preceding discussion for context. The TL;DR is that we want to allow submenu nesting on mobile too and as such we don't want a full-width menu on mobile. We also don't want to allow clicks on canvas while the mobile menu is open, as it can be especially aggravating when the welcome screen is shown and you "fat finger click" behind the menu and open something you never intended to. So the solution we agreed on with @dwelle was that:
- on mobile, stick with a non full width main menu to allow nesting/submenus
- on mobile, show a backdrop while the main menu is open; clicking on the backdrop should close the menu
- on desktop, no such restriction is needed

one issue I'm seeing is we now need to be mindful to expect and check if the element doesn't already exist, retrieve it if it does, and merge it with the updates before replacing.

I mean, we _should_ be doing that either way, but the way it's written now is implicit and implemented in an ad-hoc way. No guarantee against regressions as we add more cases or types.

I don't have a solution right now, so let's circle back to this one we refactor the file a bit to address the other issues as the solution may become clearer.

author: ad1992

body:
> if that's the case and we don't care about the user potentially overriding the properties we've properly re-calculated, then we should align all the other cases where we spread the original element?

Can you elaborate on this ?

> One other thing that may not happen now, but could happen later: we're not stripping the custom properties when we spread the objects.

Where I am calling `newElement`, `newLinearElement` etc APIs there it strips off so we need not worry however there are two places where I am not doing thats regular text and shapes (rectangle, ellipse, diamond) and we are not supporting any custom attributes in these cases so it doesn't matter but I can strip of custom attributes if passed mistakenly eg passing `start` for `rectangle`
author: ad1992

body:
> Can you elaborate on this ?

https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/blob/06dd6de0aff19f1728d55fee6312a3d5d6e6d03d/src/data/transform.ts#L545-L550

Granted, above it doesn't matter because we're reusing the width/height from the user supplied element, but if we later add something more, we may miss it.

But, there are other cases where when we calculate e.g. the wrapped text, you always prefer that calculated text. And I agree. That's why I think we should also prefer calculated `width`/`height` and other properties and not let them be overridden by the user.

> [...] we are not supporting any custom attributes in these cases so it doesn't matter but I can strip of custom attributes if passed mistakenly eg passing `start` for `rectangle`

Yep, hence why I said it may not happen now, but later (when we introduce custom props to generic or other elements). 

If we only care about the editor, `restore()` will take care of it. But if we care about the return type of the transformer, we may want to pass the generic elements through `newElement()` as well.

right now even if you supply a custom validator it will always allow youtube/vimeo/figma, so people wouldn't be able to disable those if needed.

We should either make supplying a custom validator automatically not use our own whitelist (thus forcing people to whitelist every domain they want), or support returning `boolean | undefined` where `boolean` forces the validator to exit early, and `undefined` to use our default lit.

body: was this intentional? I don't think it makes sense for embeddable elements to support binding text.
author: dwelle

body:
It is intentional. If the embeddable has a bound text element that will be used as the alt-text when exporting to PNG or SVG. This could be enhanced further in the future to be less of a hidden feature... Right now there are two ways you can edit the text:
1) by creating the alt-text in a separate text element, then binding it. If you want to change it, you need to unbind, change the text, and bind again.
2) you can add text like to a rectangle when the embeddable is empty. In this case the workflow is to create an empty embeddable, add text like you would to a rectangle, then set the link.
author: zsviczian

body:
To me the whole use case of using alt text for export seems niche in that it doesn't add much value and very few people would use it _even_ if it was actually discoverable.

But if we are to support alt text, we should implement it in a proper way. Reusing bound text is not only undiscoverable, but also forces these weird workflows (the [1] doesn't work for me when the embed already has a url), and may/will have unintended consequences. We'd also have to maintain binding logic to work with embeds just for this.

So let's decide if we absolutely need alt text. I'm for dropping it, but if you really insist, then either:

1. add UI for it in the link editor
2. use some syntax which would still be undiscoverable but has some logic/precedent behind it. I propose using the CommonMark syntax for link titles: `[text "title"](url)` https://spec.commonmark.org/0.30/#link-title, by which I mean users would set the alt text by appending it in quotes into the url input.

I'd start with (2) especially because I'm not bought on the benefit/cost ratio yet.
author: dwelle

body: but just to clarify, my first go to would be dropping support altogether.
author: dwelle

body: We can drop it. I also don't think many people would use it, especially as it is not even obvious the feature is there. I am ok to drop it.
author: zsviczian

body:
Yeah, so the idea for this was that it would be up to the host app whether to enable this behaviour or not.

I discussed this with @maielo and we agreed that by default any canvas interaction should disable follow mode and this behaviour should be consistent across OSS and E+ for example.

But I personally still think making this configurable makes sense in some specific circumstances. One such idea was a *classroom environment* where students may follow a teacher around the canvas. In that case we could enable view mode for the followers as well as set this flag to false so that:
- students can't interfere with the teachers actions
- follow mode can still be disconnected explicitly by clicking on the teacher's avatar again or using the X on the badge at the bottom of the editor, but at least accidental clicks/scrolls won't disconnect the student implicitly which I think might be the desired behaviour in this instance

But if we think this is not really necessary and we don't really want to make this configurable, I'm not against removing it.

For this purpose we introduced a new type [`ExcalidrawElementSkeleton`](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/blob/master/src/data/transform.ts#L133). This is the simplified version of [`ExcalidrawElement`](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/blob/master/src/element/types.ts#L134) type with the minimum possible attributes so that creating elements programmatically is much easier (especially for cases like binding arrows or creating text containers).

Footer is the parent component so there should be a top level class `footer` and the sub components should start with the `footer-center`, `footer-top` etc same as comp / file name convention. 

Naming it differently with some top-level parent is more confusing so I think we should keep it as is and refactor later as needed.

body:
We use BEM so it should be `footer__left`, `footer__center`, etc.

You can make an argument that `FooterCenter` is its own component so it should be `footer-center`, but this separation is somewhat arbitrary in my opinion. We can make `footer__left` into its own component any time as well. At the end of the day, the CSS naming scheme should come down to DOM structure and layout, not JSX separation.

body:
We don't clear the history anymore, instead we enable the user to go back even after starting collab. History is local to each user, so it won't expose any sensitive data until undo / action is triggered purposefully by the user. Otherwise the inability to undo inch such cases is unexpected and could lead to loss of data.

Related tests in `collab.tests.tsx` extended to cover these cases.

body:
In the perfect world we wouldn't have to capture & calculate the changes, but emit them directly to the store and let the store update the state. Bottom line for the tradeoff here is the rewrite complexity, as treating every single state update (even observable) as a command/event with payload and emitting it to the store isn't doable in one change.

Instead we we will have to reach that point incrementally & with this step we will be able to do it. In the end, we might also end up rewriting just some portions, such as hot paths due to perf. (i.e. to exchange uncommited increments of ephemeral actions) or unreliable parts (so we don't capture changes we don't want to capture, and don't end up in incosistent state after applying them).
author: Mrazator

body:
I rechecked reference props of other elements and it seems that we don't need have do this kind of merging (not to override other clients changes) in other cases, at least not now.

Closest next thing might be merging of linear element `points` - in case multiple clients modify the same linear  element and later undo / redo is perfomed to apply just changes. ATM one client will completely override the other clients changes, but it's even questionable if we should perform merge in such case as it might lead to completely broken linear element. Preferring one over the other throws the other clients changes away, but at least it leads to a consistent state which was define by one of the clients.

In the future, we might consider a special runtime brand or a decorator, which we would put over a given instance and it would automatically postprocess it (getting just the changes) & automatically merge the changes when applying it.
author: Mrazator

body:
As stated in https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/pull/7348#discussion_r1521718445, we might offer a custom decorator / brand to merge extract just the changes of reference types, such as `customData` (perhaps based on some id) and merge only changes. However, right now it does not seem to be needed, so let's not over-engineer. 

For now basic shallow compare will be performed on the first level of `customData` props, if a difference will be found, whole `customData` will be recorded as part of the increment, in the state before (`deleted`) and after (`inserted`).
author: Mrazator

Actually bound elements cannot be generic, due to keeping both bound text elements and arrows as part of `boundElements` array - hence the necessity to unbind existing text bindings, once a new text binding is added as part of the increment.

We should likely separate bound text into a standalone property, so it does not collide. It also sorts of resembles parent-child relationship, as opposed to arrow bindings.

Related https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/pull/7348#discussion_r1521718445

body: Considering it is scarcely used (currently on `boundElements` only), it would not likely result in any perf. benefits. Actually it might be even worse, since the idea behind `Delta` is that it works on plain array / object, which do not have to be instantiated on load (i.e. when restoring from storage, receiving from other clients, etc.). Also, if we would instantiate Map here for each array, we would have to then go back and forth between the Map and Object, since all the `Delta` utility methods, such as `getLeftDifferences`, `getRightDifferences` work on top of objects.

Adding element at the end is our default behaviour, which essentially leads to left-skewed indices due to halving. We should not have problems with length as the upper bound (as opposed to IEEE754 variation), but still I am interested how long the indices could get:
- length of last non-jittered index - for dev I would even prefer more readable base36 character set
- length of last jittered index ending with default base62 set (should be much shorter, but what jitter size do we expect - i.e. could we afford to extend it to limit collisions?)

body:
This is questionable, since our remote elements got already restored indices (as part of restore) & were re-ordered (in reconciliation). Therefore for our multiplayer cases this is unnecessary. This means, that for most of our cases it adds one meaningless loop over all the elements. Other options we have:
* add `isRemoteUpdate` flag to `updateScene` to distinguish between remote and local updates which go through a different process and call `restoreFractionalIndices` only on local udpates
* force the host apps and local `updateScene` cases to always call `restoreElements` (which essentially calls `restoreFractionalIndices`) - this sounds weird, but goes in line with the logic of expecting already reconciled elements in `updateScene`
* add `reorderedElements` option to `updateScene` similar to what we are doing inside `replaceAllElements`, so that we don't accidentally override already correct indices with `restoreFractionalIndices`, but just fix the incorrect ones with `fixFractionalIndices` - problem is that this exposes internal behaviour outside
* leave as it is and potentially refactor away once we will be exchanging just changes between clients (or whatever else we will be doing)

For simplicity I would go with the last option for now, as none of them is ideal.

body:
As a interim abstraction we can keep this, but the idea (as I have understood that you meant) is to make these into instance methods, not static methods. Static methods still rely on global state. The goal is to remove the element:scene mapping and thus global state.

So ultimately we want all those helpers that currently (in this PR) use these static methods to be pure (= not use global Scene state) and instead be passed elements Map or something and use that. And _then_ we'd add Scene instance methods that would use those pure helpers, passing down the state as needed.

body:
previously we were refreshing only non-containerized text elements because we were afraid (and still are) that reflowing a containerized text could end up changing the container dimensions across different browsers (clients).

Instead, I decided to not refresh text dimensions at all in this function, and just invalidate all text elements (including bound text) so as to force re-render.

@Mrazator but, we have to come up with a strategy for whether or when should we refresh text dimensions. Especially with respect to the coming virgil update which will have slightly different text dimensions. In that case, do we want to refresh dimensions, or is it ok not do it and leave it only after users edit the text later (if at all).

---

But, I'm actually not sure about this even in the current case, because it may happen the virgil doesn't load for some reason and fall back to a serif font with wildly different dimensions. Should we then refresh dimensions or leave bad bounding boxes? Tough call.
author: dwelle

body:
As discussed on Discord, updating here:

1. We will keep old Virgil for backwards compatibility.
2. "it may happen the virgil doesn't load for some reason and fall back to a serif font with wildly different dimensions" - if we don't want to refresh dimensions because we are afraid of breaking container dimensions or the overall layout; then I would also not refresh dimensions at all if we are on a fallback font

## Useless regular-expression character escape

The escape sequence '\.' is equivalent to just '.', so the sequence may still represent a meta-character when it is used in a [regular expression](1).

[Show more details](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/security/code-scanning/21)

body: > do you recall if elements being dragged should also prevent writing to history? This condition prevents triggering undo / redo, not preventing "writing to history" (recording) itself. Cases: 1. When elements are being dragged and the undo / redo won't be prevented, triggered undo / redo could be immediately overridden by the dragging element/s, possibly leading to some inconsistent states. 2. A similar case was with preventing undo / redo when there is active selection with selection element. If we are now decoupling `draggingElement`, we should again consider preventing triggering undo / redo in both of these case. > elements being dragged should also prevent writing to history About this specifically, in general, we prevent capturing store increments (~recording history entries) by default and enable it only in certain cases. There isn't any special logic around `draggingElement` which wasn't there already. One of those things that was there already, is i.e. checking `draggingElement` for scheduling capturing of increments, such as:

body:
Possibly, though nothing huge. Two reasons come in mind:

1. The `scene` contains old elements before the history increment is applied. Instead, `elements` here are new/next instances with already applied history increments. Thus, technically speaking, relying on the previous `scene.elements` (as that seems to be the only reason the scene is used here), instead of the next `elements` seems confusing (supposing not indented?) and could cause some unexpected issues in the future.
2. Another reason to have a `change` module `scene`-independent is its foreseen server-side usage (i.e. for versioning purposes), as server-side we won't likely have the individual `Scene` instances (i.e. similarly how we don't rely on a scene in `export*` utils).

body:
Thanks for the submission! This is good first step, though probably not enough, reasons:

1. there are several `insertImageElement` usages, likely covering different use cases in addition to simple paste (didn't check in detail); each of those should be manually tested and possibly captured with history - those that should be captured should have respective tests in `history.test.tsx`, those that shouldn't be captured, should have a comment why they shouldn't be captured
2. images are special types of elements, as they are loaded async, so we really have to make sure that we capture in the history just what we want (i.e. any "concurrent" update performed in the meantime, such as from collab, or from a different action, should not be included in the captured increment)
3. I am sure this regressed (at least partially) as part of https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/pull/7348, as during the PR some deployments behaved similarly as your PR, though I am not sure why, as it seems we never captured increments (~recorded changes) here. We should check how the history with images worked before the PR, how it worked in some deployments during PR, and how it regressed, so it won't regress in the future - I can hopefully help here a bit.

Also, atm there is a bug when pasting multiple times (could be related to point 2.):

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ef551361-b375-47f7-aa55-a4ed42b1a58e
author: Mrazator

body:
We should likely have multiple tests just for the simple paste like:
- pasting multiple images in a row
- performing other sync actions while the image is loading

and we should also extend each such test with additional checks, like:
- check the elements and their expected properties after applying the changes, not just that there is something unknown in the stack
- iterate the stack multiple times (undo, redo, undo), and on each check everything again

body:
## Useless regular-expression character escape

The escape sequence '\\.' is equivalent to just '.', so the sequence may still represent a meta-character when it is used in a [regular expression](1).

[Show more details](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/security/code-scanning/23)
author: github-advanced-security

body:
## Useless regular-expression character escape

The escape sequence '\\-' is equivalent to just '-', so the sequence may still represent a meta-character when it is used in a [regular expression](1).

[Show more details](https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/security/code-scanning/25)

body:
line and arrow will not work without providing the `points` property for them with at least two distinct point in it. Please note that `points` contain local points, meaning that they are relative to the element's x and y property. I.e. the first local point is (almost) always [0,0]. In your case I'm guessing the second point will be [0, 10]?

Also you need to use `pointFrom(X, Y)` to create points, you can't just add a two element tuple. Just search for pointFrom in this file to see how it is used.
author: mtolmacs

body:
There is a function to do distance calculation for 2D points, it's `pointDistance(p1, p2)`. Please convert the clientXY to a point with `pointFrom<GlobalPoint>(event.clientX, event.clientY)` and and similar with lastPointerDownEvent.clientXY, and use pointDistance on them to get the value.

We are refactoring math functions for stronger type guarantees and these cumbersome conversions will go away eventually.

body:
First of all, the element `points` is a read-only property, so ideally you'd add this when you call `newElement({ ..., points: [....]})`. 

Given that not all types of elements have a `points` property, this approach will also guide you to solve this problem you mention above, because when you provide parameters to newElement(...) you are forced to define the `type` of the element being created, which will properly validate when `points` can be added to a new element.

body:
Great question, so this might just be a MacOS thing from what I read online, but `onContextMenu` on MacOS happens "onMouseDown" if that makes sense. So in the event that we might pan, we cancel early, and if we did indeed not pan, then open up the menu with 
```
this.handleCanvasContextMenu(
            event as React.MouseEvent<HTMLElement | HTMLCanvasElement>,
          );
```
effectively converting the opening of a context menu into an `onClick`/`onMouseUp` like the many other applications that have this behavior (it matches Figma for example).

body:
@ryan-di @dwelle just came to check from https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/issues/9317#issuecomment-2765627625, which we will need to resolve in case we don't have a fallback to the main thread, but I noticed a possibly bigger issue:

This worker does not seem to be created as a separate chunk, which means it currently does not seem to be working when excalidraw is used as a package i.e. inside the browser example https://excalidraw-package-example-git-ryan-di-lasso-excalidraw.vercel.app/.

I strongly suggest to re-use the existing infrastructure for dynamically loading worker chunks.

https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/blob/0cd5a259ae6e6db87fa5af2b0466426d6920bb45/packages/excalidraw/subset/subset-main.ts#L78

Basically the content of the whole `subset-main.ts` could be recycled here - replacing the subsetting logic with lasso relevant logic and using `WorkerPool` as a light abstraction over the workers API. Similarly, `subset-worker.chunk.ts` for the pure worker chunk - juts locates the worker and calls `subset-shared.chunk.ts`, which is a separate chunk either loaded by a worker or by the main thread.

The workers infrastructure was added as part of the https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/pull/8530, more details are available there.

body:
The original code tried to offset the arrow point with a delta by modifying the points array. This is not supported for a long time, yet it didn't surface as an error until recently. 

The proper way to offset the arrow start point is to modify the element `x` and `y` properties, which is what the `LinarElementEditor.getNormalizedPoints()` does. It also offsets the rest of the points by the difference from `points[0]`. So we end up with a properly formatted array element, with the first point having a `LocalPoint` = `[0,0]`. Which also fixed the issue #9292.

I don't know the exact issue the delta offset tried to solve, but the comments lend some credibility that this offset might be needed in some edge cases. Didn't investigate further, so this PR just fixes the offsetting method, not attempting to change the behavior.

body: First of all, thanks for the PR! About this comment - interesting! I don't recall why I added it, as I would prefer to have generic family names specified after the more specific fonts first. The reason is that we don't have control over the generic font, meaning for some users, it could override the glyphs they see. I.e. if generic family fallbacks to a font which specifies CJK characters, the user wouldn't be able to see "Xiaolai" style CJK glyphs at all. --- **Question 1:** What does Segoe UI Emoji actually fallback to on Windows? On MacOS the actually rendered font seems to be "Times" (bottom right corner). JFYI you'll need to turn on "Emulate a focused page" in Chrome DevTools, so that textarea content won't disappear when changing focus. --- **Question 2**: What actually happens when you put the generic family name as the last thing in the fallback chain?

sachintha-lk

body:
I changed the Lilita One's fallback to Serif. 
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/614027a5-0ed7-4da0-912a-92ff89d7d529)

It turns out that even when we set sans-serif as the fallback some symbols fallback to a serif (At least in my system). `∑ ∞ ∫ π` are the only symbols out of the symbols I tried that properly displayed in sans-serif. 

Feel free to check it out yourself: https://excalidraw-716opavqw-excalidraw.vercel.app/#room=ae083c7aba48806a612e,OmlPh8Mdw1r8lGmGcXirIg

We can compare the sans-serif vs serif for these 4, for Math the serifs look more formal.
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6a75f536-730d-4b6f-be16-ac671eb90e2a)

When it comes to consistency the Lilita One+Serif fallback is the most consistent, but the only thing that bugs me a little is that Lilita One's comma `,` being big and bold.

The sans-serifs are not consistent as some of the symbols still rendered in serif. Out of the sans serifs, Nunito+sans-serif is more consistent than Excalifont+sans-serif because Excalifont renders custom ∑ and π
 
We could also swap the fallbacks so that Nunito get serif and Lilita One gets sans-serif, here's what that would look like
![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7b08c43e-ab70-4d4b-bb62-85639e515bcb)

I think considering **consistency** and formality, serif is the way to go for Math symbols, but we could leave a sans-serif so people can use it if they want to.
author: sachintha-lk

body:
> I think considering consistency and formality, serif is the way to go for Math symbols, but we could leave a sans-serif so people can use it if they want to.

I agree, though if we don't have a serif font (yet), I wouldn't add a serif font fallback just for the math symbols to be displayed more nicely.

I think we should add a separate serif font for a more formal style, in which case we could also add a regular serif fallback for math. This font might also complement our LateX / MathJax font, which is, for now, available just on https://math.preview.excalidraw.com/. 

Hence, in this PR, I would add a sans-serif fallback to all sans-serif fonts (including Lilita), monospace for monospace fonts, and later (other PR) we can add a specific serif font.

body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_

**Don’t convert the `LinearElementEditor` instance into a plain object**

Spreading the class instance (`{ ...linearElementEditor }`) drops all prototype-defined methods, turning it into a plain object.  
Down-stream code (e.g. selection overlays, keyboard handlers) expects `selectedLinearElement` to remain an **instance** with working methods such as `handlePointerUp`, `getPointAtIndexGlobalCoordinates`, etc. After this spread those calls will blow up at runtime.

```diff
-          appState: {
-            selectedLinearElement: {
-              ...linearElementEditor,
-              selectedPointsIndices: null,
-            },
+          appState: {
+            selectedLinearElement: (() => {
+              // keep the reference intact and just reset the field
+              linearElementEditor.selectedPointsIndices = null;
+              return linearElementEditor;
+            })(),
```

Alternatively, create a fresh `LinearElementEditor`:

```ts
selectedLinearElement: new LinearElementEditor(
  linearElementEditor.element,
  arrayToMap(newElements),
),
```

Either way, avoid the spread.

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
In packages/excalidraw/actions/actionFinalize.tsx around lines 71 to 77, avoid
spreading the LinearElementEditor instance into a plain object as it removes
prototype methods needed downstream. Instead of using the spread operator on
linearElementEditor, create a new LinearElementEditor instance by calling its
constructor with the appropriate element and updated elements map. This
preserves the class methods and prevents runtime errors.
```

</details>

body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_

**`lastCommittedPoint` becomes stale after trimming the provisional point**

When you remove the last (preview) point you only mutate `points`, leaving
`lastCommittedPoint` referring to the *old* array tail.  
This desynchronisation later breaks the “resume drawing” heuristics and causes
unexpected extra points when the user re-edits the element.

```diff
-        scene.mutateElement(element, {
-          points: element.points.slice(0, -1),
-        });
+        const newPoints = element.points.slice(0, -1);
+        scene.mutateElement(element, {
+          points: newPoints,
+          lastCommittedPoint: newPoints[newPoints.length - 1] ?? null,
+        });
```

Updating both fields keeps the editor’s internal invariants intact.  



<!-- suggestion_start -->

<details>
<summary>📝 Committable suggestion</summary>

> ‼️ **IMPORTANT**
> Carefully review the code before committing. Ensure that it accurately replaces the highlighted code, contains no missing lines, and has no issues with indentation. Thoroughly test & benchmark the code to ensure it meets the requirements.

```suggestion
        const { points, lastCommittedPoint } = element;
        if (
          !lastCommittedPoint ||
          points[points.length - 1] !== lastCommittedPoint
        ) {
          const newPoints = element.points.slice(0, -1);
          scene.mutateElement(element, {
            points: newPoints,
            lastCommittedPoint: newPoints[newPoints.length - 1] ?? null,
          });
        }
```

</details>

<!-- suggestion_end -->

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
In packages/excalidraw/actions/actionFinalize.tsx around lines 153 to 160, when
trimming the last provisional point from the element's points array, also update
the lastCommittedPoint to reflect the new last point in the trimmed array. This
prevents desynchronization between points and lastCommittedPoint, maintaining
internal consistency and avoiding issues with resuming drawing. Modify the
scene.mutateElement call to update both points and lastCommittedPoint
accordingly.
```

</details>

#### From `packages/element/src/linearElementEditor.ts` (lines 88-1864)

This file exports the `LinearElementEditor` class, which is heavily used in `actionFinalize.tsx`. Key relevant details:

- **`LinearElementEditor.handlePointerUp(event, editingLinearElement, appState, scene): LinearElementEditor`**  
  Handles pointer up events on a linear element being edited. Returns an updated `LinearElementEditor` instance with updated bindings and selected points.  
  - Parameters:  
    - `event`: PointerEvent  
    - `editingLinearElement`: current `LinearElementEditor` instance  
    - `appState`: current app state  
    - `scene`: current scene  
  - Returns: updated `LinearElementEditor` instance  
  - Implementation details:  
    - Gets the element from the scene.  
    - If dragging and points selected, checks if the path is a loop and adjusts points accordingly.  
    - Suggests bindings for start/end points if binding is enabled.  
    - Updates selected points indices based on shift key and dragging state.

- **`LinearElementEditor.getPointAtIndexGlobalCoordinates(element, indexMaybeFromEnd, elementsMap): GlobalPoint`**  
  Returns the global coordinates of a point at a given index (supports negative indices for counting from the end). Used in `actionFinalize` to get the last point's global coordinates.

- **`LinearElementEditor.getElement(id, elementsMap): ExcalidrawLinearElement | null`**  
  Static method to retrieve a linear element by id from the elements map.

- **`LinearElementEditor` constructor**  
  Takes a linear element and elements map, normalizes points if needed, and initializes properties like `startBindingElement` and `endBindingElement`.

- **`LinearElementEditor` instances hold `startBindingElement` and `endBindingElement` properties**  
  These are used in `actionFinalize` to bind or unbind linear elements.

- **`LinearElementEditor` is used to represent the editing state of a linear element, including selected points, dragging state, and bindings.**

---

- **`bindOrUnbindLinearElement(linearElement, startBindingElement, endBindingElement, scene): void`**  
  Binds or unbinds a linear element's start and end points to other elements in the scene.  
  - Parameters:  
    - `linearElement`: the linear element to bind/unbind  
    - `startBindingElement`: element to bind start to, or null/"keep"  
    - `endBindingElement`: element to bind end to, or null/"keep"  
    - `scene`: the scene object  
  - Implementation: updates bound elements and mutates elements accordingly.

- **`maybeBindLinearElement(linearElement, appState, pointerCoords, scene): void`**  
  Attempts to bind a linear element's end point to a hovered element if binding is enabled and conditions are met.  
  - Parameters:  
    - `linearElement`: the linear element to bind  
    - `appState`: current app state  
    - `pointerCoords`: coordinates of pointer  
    - `scene`: the scene object  
  - Implementation: checks hovered elements and binds if appropriate.

  Returns whether binding is enabled in the current app state.

@mtolmacs You're absolutely right. The previous conclusion was incorrect. After reviewing the codebase, I can see that `lastCommittedPoint` isn't meant to be automatically updated when trimming points.

The `lastCommittedPoint` property specifically tracks points that have been committed through user interaction (typically on pointer up events), rather than simply mirroring the last point in the array. This is intentionally separate from the points array to distinguish between committed points and provisional/temporary points.

The current code correctly preserves the actual last committed point while removing provisional points that haven't been properly committed yet. Changing this behavior would break the drawing mechanics by incorrectly marking points as "committed" when they weren't.

body: why do we import from `@excalidraw/excalidraw` here? And more importantly, we shouldn't since this isn't public API?
author: dwelle

body: Test was moved to `@excalidraw/element`. It won't affect the npm publish, since tests are not part of the bundle. What else would you do? I don't like the idea of having another type of import going directly into src (i.e. `../src/excalidraw`). Ideally the test should not depend on `@excalidraw/excalidraw` at all. Note that for `src/**/*` we have this rule in place, so that you couldn't depend on `@excalidraw/excalidraw` in src, but we tolerate it in tests for now.

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR adds a Loop Lock feature for line elements that allows users to easily toggle a closed-loop connection when drawing shapes. Key changes include introducing new icons for loop lock states, updating element action definitions and type definitions, and implementing loop lock behavior in the linear element editor.

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 8 out of 9 changed files in this pull request and generated no comments.

<details>
<summary>Show a summary per file</summary>

| File                                               | Description                                                              |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| packages/excalidraw/components/icons.tsx           | Added new icons (LoopLockedIcon and LoopUnlockedIcon) for loop lock state.|
| packages/excalidraw/components/Actions.tsx         | Added loop lock action visibility based on element type, point count, and loopLock consistency. |
| packages/excalidraw/actions/types.ts                | Extended action types to include the toggleLoopLock action.              |
| packages/excalidraw/actions/actionLinearEditor.tsx  | Registered toggleLoopLock action with loop lock behavior logic; added predicate and PanelComponent updates. |
| packages/element/src/types.ts                       | Introduced the ExcalidrawLineElement type with loopLock property.         |
| packages/element/src/typeChecks.ts                  | Added isLineElement type-check helper.                                   |
| packages/element/src/linearElementEditor.ts         | Updated linear editor logic to handle loop lock disabling when start or end points are modified. |
| packages/common/src/constants.ts                    | Defined MIN_LOOP_LOCK_DISTANCE to support the loop lock feature.         |
</details>


<details>
<summary>Files not reviewed (1)</summary>

* **packages/excalidraw/locales/en.json**: Language not supported
</details>

<details>
<summary>Comments suppressed due to low confidence (2)</summary>

**packages/excalidraw/actions/actionLinearEditor.tsx:125**
* The predicate condition uses a check for points.length >= 3, but the PR specification requires a minimum of 4 points for the loop lock action. Please change the condition to check for points.length >= 4.
```
selectedElements.every((element) => isLineElement(element) && element.points.length >= 3,)
```
**packages/excalidraw/actions/actionLinearEditor.tsx:185**
* In the PanelComponent condition, the points length check is >= 3, which conflicts with the requirement of at least 4 points. Please update this check to points.length >= 4 for consistency.
```
selectedElements.every((element) => isLineElement(element) && element.points.length >= 3,)
```
</details>

## Pull Request Overview

This PR adds a new Loop Lock feature for line elements to allow users to create closed loops by connecting the start and end points when certain criteria are met.  
- Added LoopLockedIcon and LoopUnlockedIcon to visually represent the loop lock state.  
- Updated the element actions and editors to conditionally display and handle the toggling of the loop lock state.  
- Extended type definitions and added constants to support the new behavior.

### Reviewed Changes

| packages/excalidraw/actions/types.ts | Updates action type definitions to include toggleLoopLock. |

| packages/element/src/types.ts | Defines a new ExcalidrawLineElement type with a loopLock property. |
| packages/element/src/typeChecks.ts | Adds a type check helper for line elements. |
| packages/element/src/linearElementEditor.ts | Adjusts linear element editing logic to disable loop lock if start/end points are modified. |

body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR introduces a more FP-idiomatic approach to element filtering by adding a low‐level helper (filterElements) and refactoring existing functions to leverage it. Key changes include:
- Implementation of the generic filterElements helper in utils.ts.
- Refactoring of getFrameChildren in frame.ts to use filterElements with enhanced type parameters.
- Updated usage in dragElements.ts to build element update sets with the new filtering approach.

### Reviewed Changes

Copilot reviewed 4 out of 4 changed files in this pull request and generated 2 comments.

| File                                          | Description                                                         |
| --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| packages/element/src/utils.ts                 | Added filterElements helper with generic type support and accumulator handling. |
| packages/element/src/frame.ts                 | Updated getFrameChildren to use filterElements and generics for better flexibility. |
| packages/element/src/dragElements.ts            | Refactored dragSelectedElements to leverage the new helper for filtering frames. |
| packages/common/src/utility-types.ts           | Extended utility-types with new definitions to support filtering accumulators. |


<details>
<summary>Comments suppressed due to low confidence (1)</summary>

**packages/element/src/utils.ts:389**
* The double type assertion using 'as any as' could hide potential type issues and reduce type safety; consider refining the type conversion approach to avoid extensive casting.
```
return accumulator as any as OutputAccumulator<AccumulatorType, PredicateOutputType, Attr>;
```
</details>

body:
I think the direction is fine, it's just not that easy to read, without going into `getFrameChildren` and `filterElements`, to see what they are actually doing. It seem it tries to do way too many things (frame filter from selected elements, frame children filtering, deduplication).

> // update frames and their children
- it does not seem to update anything, it's a query

> filterElements(selectedElements, isFrameLikeElement, new Set(), "id")
- I would drop the `id` attr, it's just adding unnecessary noise
- I would consider dropping the accumulator (Set and Map) to simplify the function and deduplicate in `filterElements` by default

> new Set(selectedElements)
- seems like the `selectedElements` here play no role, other than being the initial value and consequentially performing deduplication
- I would consider deduplicating selectedElements with `frameChildren` in a separate step to separate responsibilities and improve readability

That said, it would likely be slightly less efficient, but still better than the current state.

Also, I think in the future we should have the ability to query elements in the scene by various query params, and possibly have smarter db-like indices, rather than just relying on index by `id` or going always through the whole array - to further improve query/filter efficiency.
author: Mrazator
Mrazator

body:
> Also, I think in the future we should have the ability to query elements in the scene by various query params, and possibly have smarter db-like indices, rather than just relying on index by id or going always through the whole array - to further improve query/filter efficiency.

You mean [something like this](https://github.com/kofrasa/mingo)?
author: mtolmacs

body:
> it does not seem to update anything, it's a query

// [we want] to update frames and their children :)

> I would drop the id attr, it's just adding unnecessary noise

the `id` attr means we want to accumulate `id` attributes as opposed to the element objects

> I would consider dropping the accumulator (Set and Map) to simplify the function and deduplicate in filterElements by default

it's not about de-duplication (in the `dragElements.ts` it is, but the `filterElements` is generic), but about what data structure output we want. Different callers need different things.

> seems like the selectedElements here play no role, other than being the initial value and consequentially performing deduplication
> [...]
> That said, it would likely be slightly less efficient, but still better than the current state.
> [...]
> ```ts
>  const selectedFrameChildren = getFrameChildren(
>     scene.getNonDeletedElements(),
>     filterElements(selectedElements, isFrameLikeElement)
>   );
>   const deduplicatedSelectedElements = new Set([...selectedElements, ...selectedFramesChildren])
> ```

Yep, it's exactly because of performance and versatility. The refactor in `dragElements.ts` is just an example on the versatility part, we can use it however we like.

But we def wanna optimize perf on the hot path, so spreading into a new set is something I'd not do just to get a little better readability.

If we were to split it into two variables, you'll get both readability (well, the accumulator is still a bit unreadable, for sure) and performance:

```ts
const frameIds = filterElements(selectedElements, isFrameLikeElement, new Set(), "id");
const elementsToUpdate = getFrameChildren(
  scene.getNonDeletedElements(),
  frameIds,
  new Set(selectedElements),
)
```

We can jump on a call later to discuss some of the design decisions.

Again, this PR isn't something we need to do, but IMO it's an improvement over status quo. Perhaps the `getFrameChildren()` rewrite was a bit too much for our liking, and we should just ship the generic `filterElements()`?

> Also, I think in the future we should have the ability to query elements in the scene by various query params, and possibly have smarter db-like indices, rather than just relying on index by id or going always through the whole array - to further improve query/filter efficiency. (@Mrazator )

> You mean something like this? (@mtolmacs)

Yeah, although I wonder if we can afford doing all the indexing in each frame. I haven't read the library source code but I doubt it's optimized for that kind of use.
author: dwelle

body:
> You mean [something like this](https://github.com/kofrasa/mingo)?

Perhaps, there are many lighweight in-memory dbs / stores with smart / fast / incremental query possibilities. At some point I was even thinking running SQLite in wasm (on the heavier side), as it comes not just with regular b-trees, but also r-trees for spatial indexing (i.e. for collision detection and hit-testing) - if that would even help us.

> Again, this PR isn't something we need to do, but IMO it's an improvement over status quo. Perhaps the getFrameChildren() rewrite was a bit too much for our liking, and we should just ship the generic filterElements()?

Yup, sounds good.

body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_

**Incorrect value type used when `attr` is supplied**

`OutputAccumulator` currently inserts the *property name* (`Attr`) into the `Set`/`Map`, not the *property value* (`OutputType[Attr]`).  
This will resolve to e.g. `Set<"id">` instead of `Set<string>`, silently defeating type-safety and misleading consumers.

```diff
-  ? Set<[Attr] extends [never] ? OutputType : Attr>
+  ? Set<[Attr] extends [never] ? OutputType : OutputType[Attr]>
...
-      [Attr] extends [never] ? OutputType : Attr
+      [Attr] extends [never] ? OutputType : OutputType[Attr]
```

After the change, `filterElements(selected, pred, new Set(), "id")`
will correctly type-narrow to `Set<string>`.  
Failing to fix this will leak incorrect types to every call-site that uses the `attr` overload.

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
In packages/common/src/utility-types.ts around lines 87 to 93, the code
incorrectly uses the property name (Attr) as the value type in the Set and Map
accumulators instead of the property value type (OutputType[Attr]). To fix this,
replace occurrences of Attr inside Set and Map with OutputType[Attr] so that the
accumulators hold the actual property values, ensuring correct type narrowing
and type safety when an attribute is supplied.
```

</details>

body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_

**Rotation not applied to elbow-arrow collision path**

For non-elbow lines you explicitly rotate every point by `element.angle`, but for elbow arrows you return the raw path from `generateElbowArrowShape` (which is expressed in *unrotated* local coordinates).

Consequently, a rotated elbow arrow will report an axis-aligned collision shape, making hit-testing inaccurate.

Possible fix (sketch):

```diff
-  if (isElbowArrow(element)) {
-    return generator.path(generateElbowArrowShape(points, 16), options)
-      .sets[0].ops;
+  if (isElbowArrow(element)) {
+    const rawPath = generator
+      .path(generateElbowArrowShape(points, 16), options)
+      .sets[0].ops;
+
+    const center = pointFrom<GlobalPoint>((x1 + x2) / 2, (y1 + y2) / 2);
+    return rawPath.map((op, idx) => {
+      if (op.op === "move" || op.op === "lineTo") {
+        const p = pointRotateRads(
+          pointFrom<GlobalPoint>(element.x + op.data[0], element.y + op.data[1]),
+          center,
+          element.angle,
+        );
+        return { ...op, data: pointFrom<LocalPoint>(p[0] - element.x, p[1] - element.y) };
+      }
+      return op; // handle bcurveTo similarly if needed
+    });
   }
```

This keeps behaviour consistent with straight/curved arrows.

body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_

**`Math.min()` on an empty list returns `Infinity` – guard against degenerate shapes**

If a linear/freedraw element has fewer than two points, `deconstructLinearOrFreeDrawElement`
returns empty `lines`/`curves` arrays.  
`Math.min(...[])` therefore evaluates to `Infinity`, which will silently propagate into hit-testing
logic and may break threshold comparisons (`if (dist < hitThreshold) …`).

```diff
-  return Math.min(
-    ...lines.map((s) => distanceToLineSegment(p, s)),
-    ...curves.map((a) => curvePointDistance(a, p)),
-  );
+  const distances = [
+    ...lines.map((s) => distanceToLineSegment(p, s)),
+    ...curves.map((a) => curvePointDistance(a, p)),
+  ];
+  // An empty element is considered non-hittable; fall back to `Infinity`
+  return distances.length ? Math.min(...distances) : Infinity;
```

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
In packages/element/src/distance.ts around lines 133 to 138, the use of Math.min
on the combined arrays from deconstructLinearOrFreeDrawElement can result in
Infinity if both lines and curves arrays are empty. To fix this, add a guard to
check if the combined arrays are empty before calling Math.min, and return a
default large distance or a safe fallback value instead to prevent Infinity from
propagating into hit-testing logic.
```

_⚠️ Potential issue_

**Duplicate-elimination uses reference equality → incorrect inside/outside tests**

`Array#indexOf` compares *reference*, not coordinates, so two points with identical
`[x,y]` will be treated as distinct unless they share the same object.  
This can flip the *odd/even* intersection count and mis-classify points as outside
(or inside) an element.

Replace with coordinate comparison via `pointsEqual`:

```diff
-  ).filter((item, pos, arr) => arr.indexOf(item) === pos);
+  ).filter(
+    (p, idx, arr) => arr.findIndex((q) => pointsEqual(p, q)) === idx,
+  );
```

    (p, idx, arr) => arr.findIndex((q) => pointsEqual(p, q)) === idx,
  );
```
