body:
_🛠️ Refactor suggestion_

_⚠️ Potential issue_

**Intersection test ignores the dynamic `threshold` – may produce false positives/negatives**

`bindingBorderTest()` now computes a `threshold` based on `maxBindingGap`, but the subsequent call to `intersectElementWithLineSegment()` doesn’t forward this value.  
If the helper honours a distance tolerance (as every other call in this file does), omitting it will make the border test more restrictive for small elements and more permissive for large ones, ultimately breaking the “snap-to-outline” feel introduced in this PR.

```diff
-  const intersections = intersectElementWithLineSegment(
-    element,
-    lineSegment(elementCenterPoint(element), p),
-  );
+  const intersections = intersectElementWithLineSegment(
+    element,
+    lineSegment(elementCenterPoint(element), p),
+    threshold,           // keep behaviour consistent with the rest of the codebase
+  );
```

Please double-check the helper’s signature. If the third parameter is optional, passing the threshold is still harmless and future-proof.  




<!-- suggestion_start -->

<details>
<summary>📝 Committable suggestion</summary>

> ‼️ **IMPORTANT**
> Carefully review the code before committing. Ensure that it accurately replaces the highlighted code, contains no missing lines, and has no issues with indentation. Thoroughly test & benchmark the code to ensure it meets the requirements.

```suggestion
  const p = pointFrom<GlobalPoint>(x, y);
  const threshold = maxBindingGap(element, element.width, element.height, zoom);
  const shouldTestInside =
    // disable fullshape snapping for frame elements so we
    // can bind to frame children
    (fullShape || !isBindingFallthroughEnabled(element)) &&
    !isFrameLikeElement(element);
  const intersections = intersectElementWithLineSegment(
    element,
    lineSegment(elementCenterPoint(element), p),
    threshold,           // keep behaviour consistent with the rest of the codebase
  );
  const distance = distanceToElement(element, p);

  return shouldTestInside
    ? intersections.length === 0 || distance <= threshold
    : intersections.length > 0 && distance <= threshold;
};
```

</details>

<!-- suggestion_end -->

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
In packages/element/src/binding.ts around lines 1510 to 1526, the call to
intersectElementWithLineSegment does not pass the dynamically computed
threshold, which can cause incorrect snapping behavior. Review the
intersectElementWithLineSegment function signature and if it accepts a distance
tolerance or threshold as a third parameter, pass the computed threshold value
to it. This will ensure the intersection test respects the dynamic threshold and
maintains consistent snap-to-outline behavior.
```

</details>

body:
@mtolmacs I apologize for my misinterpretation of your code changes. I made an incorrect assumption about how the `intersectElementWithLineSegment` function should be used in this context, and suggested passing the threshold parameter when that wasn't the intention of your implementation.

Your new approach appears to be deliberately separating the intersection test (which checks if a line from the element center to the point crosses the element outline) from the distance check, and then combining these two pieces of information in different ways based on the `shouldTestInside` condition.

If you'd be willing to share a bit more about the reasoning behind this implementation, I'd appreciate the opportunity to better understand your approach for future reviews.

body: The binding distance must be pixel precise, so any threshold added to the bindable element would throw it off.
author: mtolmacs

body:
@mtolmacs Thank you for the clarification! That makes perfect sense - you need the geometric calculations (intersection and distance) to be pixel-precise, and the threshold should only be applied at the comparison level. Adding a threshold to the intersection calculation itself would indeed compromise the precision of the binding system.

I appreciate you taking the time to explain the reasoning behind this approach. The separation of concerns - precise geometric calculations vs. threshold-based decision making - is a much cleaner design.

---

<details>
<summary>✏️ Learnings added</summary>

```
Learnt from: mtolmacs
PR: excalidraw/excalidraw#9488
File: packages/element/src/binding.ts:1510-1526
Timestamp: 2025-05-30T19:02:50.290Z
Learning: In Excalidraw's binding system, binding distance calculations must be pixel precise. Thresholds should only be applied at the comparison level (e.g., `distance <= threshold`), not within the geometric calculation functions like `intersectElementWithLineSegment` or `distanceToElement`, as adding thresholds to these calculations would compromise the precision of the binding system.
```

body:
Not sure I follow based on your second comment. `@excalidraw/math` in my book is the most fundamental, lowest of the lowest, bottom level leaf node of the package hierarcy. It should be free of any dependency. `@excalidraw/utils` is one step further up, then `@excalidraw/common`, then `@excalidraw/elements` and finally `@excalidraw/excalidraw`.

What is your ideal hierarchy?
author: mtolmacs

body:
> @excalidraw/math in my book is the most fundamental, lowest of the lowest, bottom level leaf node of the package hierarcy

I see - in my book, that's `@excalidraw/common`, containing common utilities / constants / types shared between all other packages, which could be used by everyone, including math package.
author: Mrazator

body: From that point of view, `common/src/points.ts` should either be in `excalidraw/math` or even one layer above in `@excalidraw/element`.
author: Mrazator

body:
The `common/src/points.ts` functions should be moved to `@excalidraw/element` indeed. 

Rationale for my overall argument is that `@excalidraw/common` is sort of an "everything collection of oddities not fitting in anywhere", while `@excalidraw/math` is a narrow, specific tool doing one job (i.e. 2D vector algebra), potentially a package to even spin it off of excalidraw core for consumption in other tools. This is why I'd keep the math types in the `@excalidraw/math` package.

_🛠️ Refactor suggestion_

**Fix element type checking and error handling**

The changes properly add support for the new `rabbit-searchbox` element type in the `isExcalidrawElement` function. However, there are a couple of issues:

1. There's no case for `rabbit-image` even though it was added to `isBindableElement`
2. The switch statement contains useless case clauses as flagged by the static analysis tool


Consider refactoring the switch statement to either:

```diff
switch (type) {
  case "text":
  case "diamond":
  case "rectangle":
  case "iframe":
  case "embeddable":
  case "ellipse":
  case "arrow":
  case "freedraw":
  case "line":
  case "frame":
  case "magicframe":
  case "image":
+  case "rabbit-searchbox":
+  case "rabbit-image":
    return true;
  default: {
    console.warn(`Unknown element type: ${type}`);
    return false;
  }
}
```

This makes the code more efficient and ensures both rabbit element types are properly handled.

body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_

**`setInterval` leak – multiple timers stay alive**

A new interval is created **every** time the user toggles editing on.  
When editing finishes through *keyboard* (Escape/Enter) the pointer-up branch is never reached, leaving orphan timers that continually call `excalidrawAPI.refresh()`.

Fix by hoisting the handle and clearing in all exit paths:

```diff
-const refreshInterval = setInterval(() => { … }, 500);
-…
-if (!updatedElement.isEditing) {
-  clearInterval(refreshInterval);
-}
+if (refreshIntervalId) clearInterval(refreshIntervalId);
+refreshIntervalId = setInterval(() => excalidrawAPI.refresh(), 500);
…
+if (!updatedElement.isEditing) {
+  clearInterval(refreshIntervalId);
+  refreshIntervalId = null;
+}
```
Declare `let refreshIntervalId: ReturnType<typeof setInterval>|null = null;` outside listeners.

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
In excalidraw-app/rabbitSearchBoxHandlers.ts around lines 60 to 67, the
setInterval timer is created every time editing toggles on but only cleared in
one exit path, causing multiple orphan timers. Fix this by declaring a variable
like let refreshIntervalId: ReturnType<typeof setInterval> | null = null outside
the event listeners to hold the interval ID. Then, assign the interval to this
variable when starting the timer and ensure clearInterval(refreshIntervalId) is
called in all editing stop scenarios, including keyboard exits, to properly
clear the interval and prevent leaks.
```

</details>

body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_

**Rendering routine mutates element state → side-effects & stale versions**

`element.height` is reassigned when the text does not fit:

```ts
if (element.height < totalTextHeight) {
  element.height = totalTextHeight;
}
```

Rendering should be a pure operation.  
Mutating the element here bypasses versioning, undo/redo, collab sync, and may
trigger infinite re-renders in React.

Move the resize logic to the editing handler that owns the element’s state and
update `version` appropriately.

```diff
-  if (element.height < totalTextHeight) {
-    element.height = totalTextHeight;
-  }
+  // DO NOT mutate inside paint; return needed size to caller instead,
+  // or let editing logic resize before the frame is rendered.
```

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
In packages/element/src/renderElement.ts between lines 1181 and 1215, the
rendering code mutates element.height directly, causing side effects that break
purity and state management. Remove the logic that updates element.height here
and instead move this resizing calculation to the editing handler responsible
for element state updates. Ensure that the handler updates element.height and
increments the element's version to maintain proper versioning, undo/redo, and
collaboration sync.
```

</details>

_🛠️ Refactor suggestion_

**Consider extracting the duplicated frame handling logic.**

The frame membership update logic is duplicated between the `nextValue` and `accumulatedChange` code paths. This duplication increases maintenance burden and potential for inconsistencies.



Extract the frame handling logic into a helper function:

```diff
+const updateFrameMembership = (
+  latestElement: ExcalidrawElement,
+  scene: Scene,
+  originalAppState: AppState,
+  app: ReturnType<typeof useApp>,
+  setAppState: ReturnType<typeof useExcalidrawSetAppState>,
+) => {
+  if (isFrameLikeElement(latestElement)) {
+    const nextElementsInFrame = getElementsInResizingFrame(
+      scene.getElementsIncludingDeleted(),
+      latestElement,
+      originalAppState,
+      scene.getNonDeletedElementsMap(),
+    );
+
+    const updatedElements = replaceAllElementsInFrame(
+      scene.getElementsIncludingDeleted(),
+      nextElementsInFrame,
+      latestElement,
+      app,
+    );
+
+    scene.replaceAllElements(updatedElements);
+
+    setAppState({
+      ...app.state,
+      elementsToHighlight: nextElementsInFrame,
+    });
+  }
+};
```

Then replace both duplicated blocks with:
```diff
-      // Handle frame membership update for resized frames
-      if (isFrameLikeElement(latestElement)) {
-        // ... duplicated logic ...
-      }
+      updateFrameMembership(latestElement, scene, originalAppState, app, setAppState);
```

While this might be acceptable for error scenarios, it could lead to inconsistent behavior. packages/excalidraw/components/App.tsx (1)
`4000-4006`: **Avoid mutating the caller-supplied `elements` array** `syncInvalidIndices()` mutates the supplied array even when `shouldCreateNewInstances` is `true`. Because `sceneData.elements` originates outside of `updateScene`, this implicit mutation may surprise callers (or unit tests that reuse the same reference) and can introduce subtle bugs. A safe, low-cost guard is to clone the array before passing it to `syncInvalidIndices`: ```diff - const nextElements = elements - ? syncInvalidIndices(elements, { + const nextElementsInput = elements ? [...elements] : undefined; + const nextElements = nextElementsInput + ? syncInvalidIndices(nextElementsInput, { // we have to create new instances here, otherwise scheduled micro action below won't be able to // detect the fractional index change and won't update the store snapshot shouldCreateNewInstances: true, }) : undefined; ``` This preserves external immutability while still letting `syncInvalidIndices` mutate the working copy.
packages/excalidraw/tests/history.test.tsx (2)
`126-146`: **Rename `corrupedEntry` → `corruptedEntry` for clarity & to avoid typos elsewhere** The variable is referenced several times in this block. A consistent, correctly-spelled identifier avoids confusion and potential copy-paste errors down the road. ```diff - const corrupedEntry = HistoryDelta.create( + const corruptedEntry = HistoryDelta.create( @@ - vi.spyOn(corrupedEntry.elements, "applyTo").mockImplementation(() => { + vi.spyOn(corruptedEntry.elements, "applyTo").mockImplementation(() => { @@ - (h.history as any).undoStack.push(corrupedEntry); + (h.history as any).undoStack.push(corruptedEntry); ``` --- `405-407`: **Fix test description typo** Nitpick, but the test name is part of the public test report and IDE runner output. ```diff -it("should not push into redo stack when selection changes dooes not produce a visible change", async () => { +it("should not push into redo stack when selection changes do not produce a visible change", async () => { ```
packages/element/src/store.ts (2)
`551-566`: **Consider threading a snapshot into `StoreDelta.applyTo` to avoid data loss & extra allocations** `StoreDelta.applyTo(...)` currently invokes ```ts delta.elements.applyTo(elements); ``` which falls back to `StoreSnapshot.empty().elements` inside `ElementsDelta.applyTo`. Two minor drawbacks: 1. A fresh empty snapshot is allocated on every call – wasted work in tight undo/redo loops. 2. Fallback logic that needs the *real* previous snapshot (to resurrect force-deleted items, preserve z-order, …) is unavailable outside `HistoryDelta`. If you pipe the current snapshot through you both avoid the allocation and unlock the richer merge logic: ```diff - const [nextElements, elementsContainVisibleChange] = - delta.elements.applyTo(elements); + const [nextElements, elementsContainVisibleChange] = + delta.elements.applyTo( + elements, + snapshot.elements // <-- supply the real one + ); ``` No functional bug today, but a small performance and correctness win for future callers. --- `933-953`: **Minor readability nit – extract helpers to avoid double type-casting** The branching that prefers `editingLinearElement?.elementId` from `AppState` over `ObservedAppState` is correct, but the repeated casts clutter the code. ```ts const asState = appState as AppState; const asObserved = appState as ObservedAppState; editingLinearElementId = asState.editingLinearElement?.elementId ?? asObserved.editingLinearElementId ?? null; ``` Same for `selectedLinearElementId`. No change in behaviour, purely cosmetic.
packages/excalidraw/history.ts (1)
`142-147`: **Prefer `undefined` over `void` in union return type** `perform()` returns either a tuple or nothing. Using `void` inside a union is a bit confusing because `void` is *any* value not meant to be used. Replacing with `undefined` conveys the intent explicitly and plays nicer with strict type-checking: ```diff -): [SceneElementsMap, AppState] | void { +): [SceneElementsMap, AppState] | undefined { ``` Purely a type-level nicety, no runtime impact.

packages/element/src/delta.ts (2)
`1170-1174`: **Avoid recreating an empty `StoreSnapshot` for every delta application** Defaulting the `snapshot` parameter to `StoreSnapshot.empty().elements` allocates a brand-new `Map` on every call, even when the caller never relies on the fallback. A cheaper default keeps the contract yet skips the work: ```diff - snapshot: StoreSnapshot["elements"] = StoreSnapshot.empty().elements, + snapshot: StoreSnapshot["elements"] = new Map() as SceneElementsMap, ``` This drops a few thousand object allocations during large batch undos while behaviour stays intact (an empty `Map` is all the logic needs). --- `1325-1341`: **Guard already-filtered keys once to save iterations** Inside `applyDelta` we loop over *all* `inserted` keys and perform two `if` checks for each (`boundElements`, `excludedProperties`). For hot paths (large redo batches) we could exit early: ```diff for (const key of Object.keys(delta.inserted) as (keyof typeof delta.inserted)[]) { - if (key === "boundElements") { - continue; - } - if (options.excludedProperties.has(key)) { - continue; - } + if (key === "boundElements" || options.excludedProperties.has(key)) { + continue; + } ``` Micro-optimisation, but essentially free.

body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_

**Merge order may ignore an updated `editingLinearElementId`**  

`getObservedAppState()` prefers `editingLinearElement.elementId` over
`editingLinearElementId`.  
Because the merge spreads `snapshot.appState` **before** the incoming
`appState`, the old (possibly stale) `editingLinearElement` object survives
even when the caller only supplies a new `editingLinearElementId`.  
In that case the freshly supplied id will be silently ignored.

Please consider:

1. Reversing the merge order, or  
2. Explicitly deleting `editingLinearElement` when a new
   `editingLinearElementId` is provided.

This prevents stale ids from leaking into the observed state and ensures the
caller’s intent is honoured.

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
In packages/excalidraw/components/App.tsx around lines 4013 to 4017, the merge
order spreads snapshot.appState before the incoming appState, causing stale
editingLinearElement to override a new editingLinearElementId. To fix this,
either reverse the merge order so incoming appState properties override
snapshot.appState, or explicitly delete editingLinearElement from the merged
object when a new editingLinearElementId is present, ensuring the updated id is
respected in getObservedAppState.
```

</details>

<!-- This is an auto-generated reply by CodeRabbit -->

body: `editingLinearElementId` is not updated, that's part of the old snapshot, appState is the "updated one" hence the preference order is correct

uh, lost in the e-mails!

we only care about delta.inserted in all cases - just that will be applied

in general this reset of `crop` should never happen, as the `crop` is part of the delta only when it gets modified

I see now that `colorToRgb` is only called from `colorToRgbWithCanvas` where the input comes from `canvas.fillStyle`, which indeed normalizes and validates the color strings automatically. The canvas API ensures that `fillStyle` returns well-formed color strings, making the additional error handling unnecessary in this specific context. Thank you for the clarification!

body:
This looks ridiculous, but I don't know what else to do. I would not just ignore it, since all the async work could be slow-ish, as it's dependent on the client machine & actual image. Thus while we are initialize the image, one could move / resize / rotate / etc. the image.

And we can't just mutate, as then our image initialization could be batched with other unrelated updates, which might end up not even being captured by the store / history.

🧹 Nitpick comments (3)
packages/excalidraw/tests/history.test.tsx (2)
`614-623`: **Avoid hard-coding the pseudo‐key `id0` in delta assertions** `h.history.undoStack[0].elements.removed.id0` assumes that the delta implementation will always expose the first removed element under a sentinel key `id0`. Because `ElementsDelta.removed` is fundamentally a map keyed by real element IDs, this shortcut is brittle—any refactor that changes insertion order or skips aliasing will break the test without an actual regression. A more future-proof (and clearer) assertion pattern: ```diff -expect(h.history.undoStack[0].elements.removed.id0.deleted).toEqual( - expect.objectContaining({ /* … */ }), -); +const removed = Object.values( + h.history.undoStack[0].elements.removed, +)[0]?.deleted; +expect(removed).toEqual( + expect.objectContaining({ /* … */ }), +); ``` This keeps the test agnostic of internal bookkeeping while still validating the same contract. --- `735-744`: **Replicate the delta-key fix for the paste scenario** Same `id0` shortcut appears here. Mirror the `Object.values(…).at(0)` pattern (see previous comment) to keep both drag-and-drop and paste paths resilient to internal map key changes.

body: We probably don't want this. We would want `onSave` and `onLoad` callbacks and store the save state externally.
author: Brikaa

body:
yep, let's factor into own component, to which ideally we'd just pass `excalidrawAPI` and it'd subscribe to all necessary events inside, and render the remidner popup when applicable.

One exception being the `onLoad` event when we load the shareable link which I'd not be handling from the editor, so we'll need to come up with an API through which to notify the reminder component about it. Could be an Emitter.
author: dwelle

body:
I'd do both `(time && count)` as is now, but also trigger on `count` alone so that if user creates tons of elements fast, we still tell them to save, because they could then leave and come back after two weeks after they clear their site data (or the browser does it for them).

But we may set different element count thresholds for the "count" condition alone.
author: dwelle

body:
Hey @Brikaa, thanks for the PR!

I've gone over it and am really impressed by your understanding and use of our existing tools and APIs.

Here are some small feedbacks from me:
- personally, I think the save reminder threshold is a bit too high, with the first tier at 1 week and 300 new elements; an idea that you could consider and we could discuss: the first reminder could purely be based on the number of elements that the user has created, for example, prompt the user to save the file once over, say 20 elements have been created.
- you could probably update the description of the PR to match its current state :)
author: ryan-di

body:
candidate elements are _all_ visible elements. So taking the last element from this array won't give you the top-most element under cursor. In other words, the check for zIndex must be done _after_ all candidates for _erased_ elements are retrieved (after the loop), and also it needs to account for bound elements etc.

I'd probably start with a simple check (ignoring groups, bound elements etc.) so we can test whether the UX is actually good and we want to ship this at all.

The variable name `isArrow` suggests it should be `true` for arrow elements, but the current implementation inverts the logic.

Apply this diff to fix the logic error:

```diff

```diff

`4-4`: **Double-check that `build:packages` is resolvable from the example workspace** Vercel will run `yarn` in the example folder. packages/common/package.json (1) `3-3`: **Ensure all workspace packages are consistently bumped to 0.18.0** `@excalidraw/common` now declares `0.18.0`. dev-docs/docs/@excalidraw/excalidraw/development.mdx (1) `36-39`: **LGTM! Documentation accurately reflects the simplified release process.** The updated instructions correctly document the new unified release command that handles all preparatory changes automatically, including updating dependencies across all `@excalidraw/*` packages and the changelog. This aligns perfectly with the PR objective of streamlining the release process.
examples/with-nextjs/package.json (1) `6-7`: **LGTM! Build script adaptation maintains functionality while using unified build system.** The addition of `build:packages` and the update to `build:workspace` correctly adapts this Next.js example to use the new unified build system while preserving the existing workflow of building packages followed by copying assets.
scripts/updateChangelog.js (1) `23-36`: **LGTM! Changes align with the new multi-package release process.** The updated logic correctly searches for release commits without a specific version suffix and returns only the first (most recent) commit hash. This is appropriate for the new release workflow where all packages are released together.
packages/excalidraw/package.json (1) `69-85`: **LGTM! Package restructuring supports the new multi-package architecture.** The addition of inter-package dependencies and build scripts properly implements the modular package structure. All @excalidraw dependencies are correctly synchronized at version 0.18.0.
package.json (2) `55-59`: **Build scripts properly structured for multi-package workflow.** The sequential build order (`common` → `math` → `element` → `excalidraw`) ensures dependencies are built before dependent packages. This is crucial for the new architecture where packages reference each other directly. --- `83-86`: **Release scripts successfully unified.** The consolidated release commands provide a clean interface for different release types while maintaining flexibility through command-line arguments.
scripts/release.js (4) `8-72`: **Well-structured argument parsing with proper validation.** The function correctly handles all release scenarios and validates inputs appropriately. The automatic version suffixing with git commit hash for non-latest releases ensures unique versions. --- `118-149`: **Git operations properly scoped to latest releases.** The interactive commit flow is well-implemented, and limiting it to latest releases makes sense since test/next releases are typically automated. --- `151-165`: **Build process ensures clean, reproducible builds.** Good use of `--frozen-lockfile` and cleaning artifacts before building. The sequential build approach aligns with the dependency order. --- `204-222`: **Main execution flow is well-orchestrated.** The order of operations (build → changelog → version updates → commit → publish) ensures validation at each step before proceeding. Good separation of CI and interactive modes.

body:
_⚠️ Potential issue_

**CLI flag likely malformed – replace "`- ci`" with "`--ci`"**

A single dash followed by a space is parsed as a positional argument `"−"` and `"ci"`, not a flag.  
Unless your `scripts/release.js` explicitly expects that, this step will exit with an unknown parameter error.

```diff
-          yarn release next - ci
+          yarn release next --ci
```

<!-- suggestion_start -->

<details>
<summary>📝 Committable suggestion</summary>

> ‼️ **IMPORTANT**
> Carefully review the code before committing. Ensure that it accurately replaces the highlighted code, contains no missing lines, and has no issues with indentation. Thoroughly test & benchmark the code to ensure it meets the requirements.

```suggestion
          yarn release next --ci
```

</details>

<!-- suggestion_end -->

<details>
<summary>🤖 Prompt for AI Agents</summary>

```
In .github/workflows/autorelease-excalidraw.yml at line 27, the CLI flag "- ci"
is incorrectly formatted as a single dash followed by a space, which is treated
as positional arguments rather than a flag. Replace "- ci" with the correct
double-dash flag "--ci" to ensure it is recognized as a proper CLI option and
prevent unknown parameter errors.
```

</details>

body:
Hi @Eshan-Jogwar, appreciate the PR. 

I don't think a separate SnappingManager is needed. If you take a look at snapping.ts (some additional changes might be needed elsewhere too), you can easily extend to snap a frame to its children.

Why this is favored:
- minimal changes
- consistent logic
- snapping won't clash with each other

Do feel free to reach out to us over at discord or here on github :)
author: ryan-di

body: Hey @Krishna-mishra-26, thanks for the PR. I think its current state is very much incomplete? Cool idea though, would love to see you complete it so we can have a proper valuation at any potential perf gains. Let me know how we could help and please do keep us posted :)
author: ryan-di

body: Thanks @sohamsk13 👍
author: ryan-di

body:
First of all, thanks for the PR!

As shown in the recording, that flow with multiple selected elements & groups seems to work as expected. 

Additionally, I think it would be useful to make the align / distribute actions work when selecting a single group (and possibly selected group within a group + multiple selected groups within a group), without having to select individual items inside.

The basic flow is recorded here: https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/issues/9606#issuecomment-2963788008
author: Mrazator

body: These predicated don't work as expected. If you select a group now, without manually selecting the inner elements, it won't show the align / distribute options.
author: Mrazator

body: These predicated don't work as expected. If you select a group now, without manually selecting the inner elements, it won't show the align / distribute options.
author: Mrazator

body: I would not create the artificial groups by `element.id`, as those are not groups, which might lead into confusion .
author: Mrazator

body: I definitely see where confusion could happen, thanks for addressing....would you consider syntax change to the variable `group`, instead naming it something like `containers`?
author: cTangonan123

body: If we go the route I'm currently thinking of altering `getSelectedElementsByGroup` to check for the case in which a single `groupId` is selected and nothing else, I think it could work as the length instead would count for every element within the group...open to any suggestions you may have on this.
author: cTangonan123

body: refer to https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/pull/9721#discussion_r2193107489
author: cTangonan123

body: We have `containers` keyword reserved for text containers (elements which have bound text elements), maybe just have two separate functions and refer to each as `groups` and `elements`.
author: Mrazator

packages/element/tests/align.test.tsx (2) `622-687`: **Well-structured tests for group edit mode alignment!** The test cases comprehensively cover all alignment directions and correctly verify element positioning within groups during edit mode. --- `834-1011`: **Excellent test coverage for single group selection scenarios!** These tests thoroughly verify alignment behavior when a single group (with or without nested groups) is selected, ensuring the new grouping logic works correctly in these edge cases.

author: Mrazator

body: False positive, without mouse.reset(), the mouse starts at the previous x,y (200,200) plus the x,y (0,0) passed in mouse.down(0,0).
author: Mrazator

body:
@Mrazator, thank you for the correction! You're absolutely right - I misunderstood how the mouse API works in the test framework. I incorrectly assumed `mouse.down(0, 0)` was using absolute coordinates when it's actually relative to the current mouse position after `mouse.moveTo(200, 200)`.

This means the rectangle is indeed created at the intended position (200, 200) to (300, 300), making my suggestion a false positive.

---

<details>
<summary>✏️ Learnings added</summary>

```
Learnt from: Mrazator
PR: excalidraw/excalidraw#9721
File: packages/element/tests/align.test.tsx:710-713
Timestamp: 2025-07-15T10:40:04.145Z
Learning: In Excalidraw test files, the mouse API uses relative coordinates for mouse.down() and mouse.up() events after a mouse.moveTo() call. For example, after mouse.moveTo(200, 200), calling mouse.down(0, 0) will position the mouse at (200, 200), not at the absolute coordinate (0, 0).
```

body:
this one currently has bad UX with pen input because it's pretty much impossible to exit the line editor by pointerdown with a pen. Not sure if it's because of the pen immediately box-selecting due to being oversensitive, or if we really prevent pen events to exit line editing. So we should fix it (e.g. add bigger threshold for box-selecting to not exit the editor while in line editor if that's the root cause).

NOTE that in prod it's still hard to exit with pen, but somewhat doable. In this PR it seems impossible, but I can't see which code change in this PR might be causing this...

author: ryan-di

body:
Yeah, the current UX for using a pen in line editing mode, from what I can tell, is to allow the users to
- use the pen to box select points
- clear the selected points on pen down
- move the selected points
And it seems that we can only exit the line editing mode with our fingers. 

Not noticing any differences between prod and this PR. But will investigate a bit more.

packages/excalidraw/tests/history.test.tsx (2)
`1074-1087`: **`?? false` may hide an undefined `isEditing` field** `selectedLinearElement` should now always expose a boolean `isEditing`. By using `?? false` the test will succeed even if the field is accidentally left `undefined`, masking a potential regression. ```diff -expect(h.state.selectedLinearElement?.isEditing ?? false).toBe(false); +expect(h.state.selectedLinearElement?.isEditing).toBe(false); ``` If the property can be legitimately `undefined`, consider asserting that explicitly; otherwise trust the type contract and compare the value directly. --- `1090-1095`: **Repeated manual assertions → extract a tiny helper** The same two-line pattern ```ts assertSelectedElements(h.elements[0]); expect(h.state.selectedLinearElement?.isEditing).toBe(); ``` is duplicated a dozen times. body:
## Pull Request Overview

This PR fixes a multi-element interaction issue on touchscreen devices by adding a condition to check that the new element has more than one point before applying touchscreen-specific positioning logic.

- Adds a point count check to prevent premature positioning adjustments on touchscreen devices
- Ensures the touchscreen positioning logic only applies to elements with multiple points
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
The condition checks for `points.length > 1` but should verify that `newElement.points` exists before accessing its length property to prevent potential runtime errors.
```suggestion
          if (this.device.isTouchScreen && newElement.points && newElement.points.length > 1) {
```

body: See https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/pull/9853/files#diff-1c9157c6c99293784a7cd15cf33ed294ad7513f0a6dfcd135830f9f002a5141bL4931

body: How is it relevant in blob / svg export?
author: Mrazator

body:
My POV is that deleting empty text elements during restore could result in major bugs, i.e. making the text elements deleted forever, even when the "text" content is applied to them.

If that happens, it would be pretty bad for the user, since he would lose his textual content. If we were to do it by default on every restore, any `restore` anywhere could potentially cause this, increasing the failure surface and probability that this would happen.

On the other side, not deleting such text elements on every restore would not result in anything this bad, AFAIK we could live with such elements without facing too many issues.

Anyway, it should be pretty rare that this happens, since we are marking such elements as deleted on every text wysiwyg submit, but if that happens, I would rather live with non-deleted element than having to face a major bug like that.
author: Mrazator

body:
@Mrazator after removing the `CaptureUpdateAction.NEVER`, I noticed errored images don't cause problems with history/increments (just a bug that I introduced while refactoring, then fixed). I'm assuming it works because there has been no visible changes with the errored image between the snapshot at the time of commit and the previous snapshot.

But yeah, that's not explicit.
author: Brikaa

body:
It has to be never, since otherwise it would be eventually, which would make this change "undoable" (you could undelete the image, which is not what you want).

I was pointing to a slightly different problem when e.g. one image is initialized, while other throws and is deleted.

In that case, the `initializeImage` would have to be super fast in order to result in same component re-render as mutateElement with isDeleted:true. This would scheduleAction never and at the same time as scheduleAction immediately & immediately is preferred, so the deletion would again be "undoable".

Likely won't happen now, but perhaps in the future in some edge case it could.
author: Mrazator

body:
> It has to be never, since otherwise it would be eventually, which would make this change "undoable" (you could undelete the image, which is not what you want).

@Mrazator But, even if we don't have `never`, the store won't notify about the addition of a deleted element anyway, right? Because it is a change that is not visible:
- Previous snapshot: placeholder did not exist
- Eventual: placeholder existed as non-deleted (but snapshot is not updated)
- Current snapshot: placeholder existed as deleted (no notification since no visible change from last snapshot)
author: Brikaa

body:
The store even seems to ignore non-initialized images:
https://github.com/excalidraw/excalidraw/blob/ae89608985dfe5975b5fa0103d5aec64cb4fb83f/packages/element/src/store.ts#L937-L943

So this seems to be why it prevents undoing without `never`, rather than the because of the visible change thing I assumed.

body: “I was wondering — could you clarify why `element` is treated as a single shape in this context, rather than a group or list? I’m also curious how that design choice affects the handling of bound text. Additionally, is there a specific reason why `ArrowElement` is excluded from this logic?”
author: levi42x

body: I think it will only work when the rotated element is parallel to the axes. But what if the bound text is at some angle to the axes? Is it still considered parallel to the axes or not?
author: levi42x

body:
hey @levi42x , 
Good question! Hoping this would be helpful, as an `element` in this context, can contain `boundElements`, while text elements can contain at most 1 `containerId`. This distinction is what differentiates them from a group or list, as it allows for the various text alignments within its container.

As for the `ArrowElement`, I didn't add the exclusion myself, but my understanding is that it's intentional. Where for arrows, bound text is designed to maintain an angle of 0 and a position at the midpoint between its endpoints. If it were removed, the angle of text would change in accordance to the rotation of the multiple elements, which could break consistency.
author: cTangonan123

body:
@levi42x I think this is an instance you're referring to, even when an individual text element is rotated, when it is bound, it will start and maintain the same angle as its container.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7e8fe15b-122e-4515-a576-121d99b34bf5

body:
## Pull request overview

Adds Mongolian locale support and adjusts the WYSIWYG text editor to enable browser spellcheck (to address Edge spell-correction behavior).

**Changes:**
- Enable `spellcheck` on the WYSIWYG textarea used for text editing.
- Add `mn-MN` locale JSON file.
- Register `mn-MN` in the language list.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 3 out of 3 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| packages/excalidraw/wysiwyg/textWysiwyg.tsx | Enables spellcheck on the textarea used by the WYSIWYG editor. |
| packages/excalidraw/locales/mn-MN.json | Introduces Mongolian translations (new locale file). |
| packages/excalidraw/i18n.ts | Adds `mn-MN` to the `languages` array. |


<details>
<summary>Comments suppressed due to low confidence (1)</summary>

**packages/excalidraw/i18n.ts:74**
* `mn-MN` is added to `languages`, but it will currently be filtered out because `percentages.json` has no entry for `mn-MN` (the filter compares `undefined >= COMPLETION_THRESHOLD`). As a result the language won’t appear in the language selector despite being listed here. Add/update the `mn-MN` completion value in `packages/excalidraw/locales/percentages.json` (or adjust the filtering logic to handle new locales) so the new locale is actually selectable.
```
    { code: "zh-TW", label: "繁體中文" },
    { code: "vi-VN", label: "Tiếng Việt" },
    { code: "mr-IN", label: "मराठी" },
    { code: "mn-MN", label: "Монгол" },
  ]
    .filter(
      (lang) =>
        (percentages as Record<string, number>)[lang.code] >=
        COMPLETION_THRESHOLD,
    )
```
</details>

body:
Alt/Cmd/Ctrl etc. should also be translated. I'm thinking the translation itself should happen in `getShortcutKey()`, but that's currently a util method in a package that ideally shouldn't be importing i18n helpers.

Two options:

1) move the helper out to `packages/excalidraw`
2) introduce a second intermediate helper `parseShortcutKey` that returns normalized key code instead of string for display purposes, e.g. `CtrlOrCmd` -> `ctrl`, which we we'd then pipe into `t("keys.${normalizedKey}")`.

downside of (2) is we still want to translate the keys in other parts of the UI that currently uses `getShortcutKey()` anyway. So I'm thinking going with (1).

body:
## Pull request overview

Adds additional emoji font fallbacks and updates the font-family fallback selection logic to improve emoji rendering (especially on Apple/Android platforms).

**Changes:**
- Added `Apple Color Emoji` and `Noto Color Emoji` as fallback font constants.
- Updated `getFontFamilyFallbacks()` to conditionally prefer platform-specific emoji fonts.

### Reviewed changes

Copilot reviewed 2 out of 2 changed files in this pull request and generated 3 comments.

| File | Description |
| ---- | ----------- |
| packages/common/src/constants.ts | Adds new emoji fallback constants and updates fallback-selection logic. |
| packages/common/src/utils.ts | Updates imports to include the new emoji fallback constants. |
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
`getFontFamilyFallbacks` now references `isDarwin`, `isIOS`, and `isAndroid`, but these identifiers are not declared or imported in `constants.ts`, which will fail TypeScript compilation. Either import them from the appropriate module (ensuring any UA detection is safe in non-browser contexts), or avoid platform branching here by always returning a single ordered list of emoji fallbacks (e.g., include Windows/Apple/Android emoji fonts in a consistent order).
```suggestion

  switch (fontFamily) {
    case FONT_FAMILY.Excalifont:
      return [
        CJK_HAND_DRAWN_FALLBACK_FONT,
        genericFallbackFont,
        WINDOWS_EMOJI_FALLBACK_FONT,
        APPLE_EMOJI_FALLBACK_FONT,
        ANDROID_EMOJI_FALLBACK_FONT,
      ];
    default:
      return [
        genericFallbackFont,
        WINDOWS_EMOJI_FALLBACK_FONT,
        APPLE_EMOJI_FALLBACK_FONT,
        ANDROID_EMOJI_FALLBACK_FONT,
      ];
```
author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer

body:
`APPLE_EMOJI_FALLBACK_FONT` and `ANDROID_EMOJI_FALLBACK_FONT` are imported here but never used in this file, which will trigger unused-import linting (and adds noise). Please remove them, or update the code to actually reference them.
```suggestion

```

If the filter-related fixes are intended, they appear to be missing from the implementation; otherwise, please update the PR description to match the actual scope.

author: dwelle

body:
Firstly, this `updateBoundElements()` will only run for ExcalidrawBindableElements only. In fact the heading of that function does another early termination if the provided element is not a Bindable element.

Secondly, if you drag a bindable element, only binding elements can be connected to it and this function doesn't transitionally update elements connected on the other side of these binding (currently arrow only) elements, just the arrows. We *do* want to update all arrows when a reset like this happens, because that is the bug.

Thirdly, future-proofing-wise I think we should always let the element binding logic handle how the update should happen and all such functionality shouldn't depend on update frequency.
author: mtolmacs
